topic: Area B: Personal and Social Life
subject: french

items.q: How do you say 'I am using my phone right now' (emphasising the action is in progress)?
items.correct: Je suis en train d'utiliser mon portable.
items.why: Students translate English '-ing' as a participle. French has no progressive tense - the present 'j'utilise' covers both 'I use' and 'I am using'. For emphasis on the ongoing action, use 'être en train de + infinitive'.

items.q: How do you say 'Currently, I am using social media' in French?
items.why: Students avoid actuellement thinking it means 'actually'. It really means CURRENTLY. Useful for tech/social media topics. To say 'actually' (in reality), use 'en fait' or 'en réalité'.

items.q: How do you say 'I am eating bread' (some bread) in French?
items.random_wrong: Je mange le pain (which means 'the bread').
items.why: Students drop the partitive. French requires DU/DE LA/DES for 'some' even when English omits it. Skipping it makes the sentence ungrammatical in French.

items.q: Which sentence means 'Maths is more difficult than English'?
items.correct: Les maths sont plus difficiles que l'anglais.
items.misconception: Les maths est plus difficile que l'anglais.
items.random_wrong: Les maths sont moins difficiles que l'anglais.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Les maths sont plus difficiles que l'anglais.. “Les maths est plus difficile que l'anglais.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: les devoirs. “la récréation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many lessons typically make up a French collège school day (matin + après-midi, approximate standard)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 lessons. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
